# Compatibility Playbook

## Current Sample Size

- Candidate replies: 13
- Strong examples: 10

## Common Tags

- WF Sales
- WF Support
- MIMO Lead (FC)
- Resolved - Tech Support
- SLA Breach

- Data connections, MIMO & LTE Routers Knowledge Base (knowledge\approved\sops\Data connections, MIMO & LTE Routers Knowledge Bas 25b478fed7ef43ea8ef4e17e0daf5dbe.md)

## Example Reply Patterns

> Joey,
> 
> Thank you for your response. I've attached the T-Mobile towers in your area to this email. You do have a few towers in your area around you which can lead to interference.
> 
> I'd suggest trying the east, west, and south sides of the house at lower locations to have the walls of the house block the other towers (if you haven't tried those towers in the map). 
> 
> As you relocate/reaim the antenna, I'd suggest checking the signal metrics. To view your signal metrics, including SINR, RSRP, RSRQ and the band, please download the HINT App. You can check them as soon as you connect the app to the gateway. You may need to use the gateway's admin password to login. That information is typically found on the unit's sticker or the password for your Wifi network. I've also attached a guide on how to use the HINT app to assist.
> 
> Below are the 2 links to download the app:
> 
> Android
> 
> [url]
> 
> iPhone
> 
> [url]
> 
> Generally, in a cellular connection, the signal quality, measured by the SINR, plays a crucial role in determining data speeds. Therefore, it's vital to aim for a high and positive SINR value to achieve optimal results, ideally above 3 dB and stable. On the other hand, the signal strength, indicated by RSRP, has a relatively lesser impact on connection speeds, but it's still essential to ensure that the value is more positive than -110dBm, closer to zero for optimal performance. 
> 
> RSRQ has to be lower than -12 dBm (i.e. closer to zero like -10 dBm). If its above -12 , i.e. -13 or -14 dBm, then you would have tower congestion which, sadly, isn't controlled by the antenna, you can try aiming for another tower if that's the case. 
> 
> One final step you can try is swapping the middle ports, so if you're connected to ports 1234, try 1324.
> 
> Let me know how it goes. I'd be happy to help!
> 
> Best,

> Hi Joey,
> 
> Thank you for sharing all the details and for walking through your plan so thoroughly. I completely understand the challenge, and it sounds like you’ve put a lot of thought into making this work.
> 
> Regarding your questions:
> 
> (1) Putting the modem in a weatherproof box is something some people do when the best signal is far from the house. It can work, but keep in mind that proper ventilation and temperature control are important to avoid overheating. Make sure the box is rated for outdoor use and has some airflow. Running conduit with power and ethernet is also a common approach in setups like this.
> 
> (2) As for the signal, the SINR numbers you’re seeing give a good idea of stability. If the signal isn’t stable, the performance won’t be stable either, even from a “perfect” spot. Often, placing the antenna a little lower on your property, rather than at the very back fence, can actually help the antenna achieve a more consistent connection. It’s all about finding a balance between line of sight and stability.
> 
> Ultimately, if you can achieve a stable connection in that spot or slightly lower, your plan could work. If the SINR and signal strength fluctuate too much, it might not give the reliable performance you’re hoping for.
> 
> I hope this helps!
> 
> Best,

> Hi Joey,
> 
> Thanks for all the details, I really appreciate it.
> 
> The fluctuations in your download and upload speeds are caused by interference from nearby towers, buildings, and other signals. Your current directional antenna is actually the best choice for your setup. Switching to a non-directional antenna would likely make the fluctuations worse because it picks up signals from all directions, including more interference.
> 
> Placing the antenna higher is not necessarily better either. In your environment, going up exposes it to more interference, which can make performance less stable rather than improving it.
> 
> Given the challenges with line of sight and aesthetics, the best approach is to keep your antenna in a spot where it has the clearest path to the strongest tower while staying practical. If the front corner is too visible, you could try slightly lower positions or side locations that are less obstructed but still give a reasonable signal. Small adjustments in height and orientation can sometimes make a big difference without needing a roof installation.
> 
> If you can help me with your location address, I can check the towers and possibly check some other towers to try. Also, can you please tell me which gateway you're using?
> 
> Best,
